output: Mr. President, first of all, I would like to congratulate Mr. Sieb for the work carried out, because his report reflects many of the concerns expressed in this
House, regarding these crucial problems for the entire European Union. These are water scarcity and drought, issues that have ceased to be solely a problem for countries in Southern Europe.

I am glad that this project incorporates some of the reflections made in the opinion report for which I was the rapporteur in the Committee on Agriculture, in favor of agricultural activity, highlighting, for example, the role that farmers play in the sustainable management of available resources.

It also includes a reference to drought and water scarcity, as factors that exacerbate raw material prices. A point which I also think it's very important to highlight in current times, so that we bear in mind not only the environmental dimension of this problem, but also some of its most significant economic consequences.

The Environment Committee also included the idea of creating a European Drought Observatory, an action that is also mentioned in the report of the Agriculture Committee and which I hope will one day be reflected in concrete actions.

The text that will be voted on tomorrow does not include, however, a proposal made by the Agriculture Committee to study the implementation of an economic adaptation fund against drought, a fund that would benefit all economic sectors, including agriculture.

For my part, I would like to make it clear that I will continue to defend the establishment of this fund. It is an idea that I will propose again to Parliament when the communication that the Commission plans to publish on adaptation to climate change arrives in the coming months.

It seems to me that after the Solidarity Fund was established, which was created in its day to alleviate the losses caused by climate calamities, it is time for us to think about an instrument that acts a priori and is designed to finance prevention measures to reduce the environmental and economic cost of these climate catastrophes.

And the reality is that there is a national reality in which there are 27 countries with different legal systems and with very different languages, which requires that in each of those countries we adapt to the realities of the country, including the issue of languages.

Specifically, as Gosse has just pointed out, and as previous speakers have pointed out, it is important that the recognition of these national specialties does not lead to an increase in costs, that the fees be uniform, and that governments eventually assume that additional cost.

Specifically, in the European Union, we do not have a common language.

And from a legal point of view, furthermore, we are faced with the reality that legal documents are different.

In the Legal Commission, the Legal Commission has proposed, for example, some amendments regarding the authentication of the minutes, of the documents, along with the authentication of the translation.
At this moment, the Committee on Legal Affairs is preparing a report on the recognition of authentic acts in different countries of the European Union, which should be complementary to this report.
Ultimately, I believe that the Commission has done well in presenting this report.
The rapporteur has done a magnificent job and I believe that we have all striven to make a proposal for a legislative text that can be recognized throughout the Union, guaranteeing

rights and building upon the foundations of the reality of a union that is being built, which has different national legal frameworks and which has different levels of access to electronic communication.

Mr. President, on behalf of the socialist group, I want to express my group's most vehement rejection of the murders being committed in this region of Somalia, invoking the name of God.
Few atrocities have been committed by someone who has not invoked the name of God or of a religion.

Specifically, the way this heinous murder was committed, as we already know, a 13-year-old girl who is raped and then accused of adultery, and that five men, if they can be called men, rush to stone her and even prevent anyone from trying to save her. In a stadium occupied by a thousand people.

This, coupled with acts of piracy that this year off the coast of Somalia have reached the level of 1,000 ships—uh, sorry, of almost 100 ships seized—constitutes an absolutely inadmissible humanitarian situation.
The international community cannot remain impassive in the face of this.
One cannot remain impassive in the face of cowardice, in the face of religious invocation to commit atrocities.
Therefore, we must reestablish order by supporting the legitimate government of Somalia, to regain control of the entire country, and to establish a rule of law that respects human rights.
I believe that on few occasions we have been presented with such a clear situation and one that imposes obligations on us.
I don't think we can remain here impassive in this community of 500 million people and 27 countries, the most important in the world, without intervening in that situation.

Mr. President, in the EU there are various cases of indigenous languages of historical European peoples which, because they are not constituted as a state, are not allowed to be used in the debates of this Parliament, and this is a democratic and representative deficit.

One of these cases is the Basque language, or Euskera, which is not a minority language but an official one, at least in the southern part of the Basque Country, which is administratively part of the Spanish state.

But the same does not happen, and please don't take it personally, Mr. Commissioner, with the Northern Basque Country, attached to the French State, whose president, before the UN Assembly, states that denying respect for national identities and languages is to fuel humiliation and that without that respect there will be no peace in the world.
And then neither Basque, nor Corsican, Breton, or Occitan have the slightest official consideration nor do they receive support to respect and promote their use.

For this reason, I ask that the Fundamental Rights Agency, Verly, work so that within the member states, the rights to the mother tongue of any European people are not violated, nor are its inhabitants discriminated against, and that all of them are considered official within their respective territories.
Thank you very much, ladies and gentlemen, for your support of all European languages.

Madam President, first of all, I want to thank our rapporteur, Claudio Fava, for his great work in achieving a common position with the Council, which includes many improvements proposed by our House.
With this directive, we once again demonstrate our commitment to building a common migration policy based on a global approach.
The approach of this directive is clear: to fight against mafias, punish unscrupulous employers, and protect exploited immigrants who lack any type of social protection.
We want the unfair, meager wages for migrants to disappear, which also distort average wages, especially in sectors such as construction, agriculture, domestic service, or hospitality.
These measures require great courage and political will, as there are many vested interests and a lot of money moving in the shadow economy.
Precisely in difficult times, it is more important than ever to intelligently manage migratory flows, with generosity, but with responsibility.
It would be easy to fall into the temptation of not trying to curb the shadow economy.
We cannot look the other way and leave around 8 million irregular immigrants in a vulnerable situation, with labor conditions close to slavery.

Honourable Members, if we want this directive to be effective, rigorous inspections are necessary, along with economic and even criminal sanctions in the most serious cases, which would have a deterrent effect on businesses.
We will thus manage to reduce the volume of the informal economy and cut the migratory incentive that creates the possibility of working irregularly.
It must be clear that the only option to work in Europe is the legal way.
Therefore, we want to move forward with the global approach and we ask the Commission, Commissioner, Mr. Barrot, to present as soon as possible the new "blue cards" for the other labor categories.

In the same way that this Parliament has repeatedly requested that the European Union be equipped with the necessary instruments to speak with a single voice in the world, the Chamber itself must also show itself to be cohesive around the evaluation and promotion of the great priorities in foreign policy and common security.

peace throughout the planet or a commitment to human development. On the other hand, to propose rebalancing elements between the geographical priorities of the CFSP, regarding how they originally appeared in the text, if they appeared, or to introduce them as new elements if they were absent.

00:00 We have advocated for greater diversification and greater relevance of relations with Africa, a continent that we often only remember when wars of special virulence occur, and many times not even then.

Thank you, Mr. President. The Venezuelan referendum has resulted in a triumph for the "yes" vote, elegantly recognized by the democratic opposition.

It is no less true that there has been no equality of opportunity in the campaign and that the officialism has counted on the overwhelming support of the entire state apparatus, while the opposition has suffered constant harassment and coercion.
Even under those conditions, the country has been practically divided into two halves, and it will be very difficult to build the future with only one of them.
The European Union must promote dialogue, inclusion, and consensus among Venezuelan political and social actors for the good of the country.
It is not with visceral attitudes, disqualifications, or insults that we will help Venezuela find its democratic, pluralist, and free path.
In that sense, we criticize the decision of the Venezuelan government to expel a Spanish MEP, and even more so the manner in which such expulsion was carried out. But we call on this chamber to avoid

that our representatives make statements during their visits to third countries that violate the current local legislation, and even less that they insult a head of state, however much he may be criticized.

This is something that the commission cannot do by itself either.

are patented to know when and how many residues from bilges and fuel tanks have been illegally dumped into the sea by a specific vessel.

Mr. President, the revision of the Eurovignette directive is a matter of great importance that concerns and worries us all, especially the peripheral countries who feel like victims of this proposal.
The European Commission has decided to address the internalization of the external costs of road transport by attributing three new costs: air pollution, noise pollution, and congestion.

While the principles inspiring the proposal—that the user pays and the polluter pays—are reasonable, the solution being proposed is by no means so, as it will be discriminatory for a sector that has been suffering for months from the disastrous effects of the economic crisis.
Demonizing road freight transport, even if that wasn't the objective, has turned out to be the final outcome.

This proposal, ladies and gentlemen, is ill-timed. And far from achieving the expected objectives of sustainable transport, it would be the death sentence for a large number of small and medium-sized European enterprises that employ thousands.

people in the European Union and who contribute daily to the distribution of goods, ensuring that products reach their final consumer. A consumer who will see how their products become more expensive due to the application of these rates.
Ladies and gentlemen, we need a competitive, sustainable, and environmentally friendly transport system, without disregarding roads, because currently it is the only way to reach everywhere.
The modal shift continues to be a dream, far from being a reality.
The Trans-European networks, the motorways of the sea, the cross-border connections remain, in some cases, projects and not a reality.
Ladies and gentlemen, when the Titanic of the global economy sinks, it is not possible to ask the orchestra to keep playing, much less for joy.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, this question, as Mr. Boklan has explained, is very opportune given the diplomatic conference on the Convention on Ship Recycling that will be held in May.
Therefore, I would like to congratulate Mr. Brok, not only for the question but also for the resolution he has prepared.

In the Popular Party, we are in favor of the fight against the bad practices and methods that are being used to dismantle ships, and therefore we support Mr. Brokland's resolution proposal. But there are some points that concern us.

The first is section 3, which he has also pointed out, and which states that ships in their entirety should be considered hazardous waste, and consequently should fall within the scope of the Basel Convention.

We have submitted an amendment on this section, because there are different interpretations both inside and outside the European Union, and we understand that the Basel Convention must be taken into account and not contradicted, but it should be the future international agreement on ship recycling that defines the details of its application.
The draft resolution also calls, in point 14, for a financing mechanism based on mandatory contributions from the maritime transport sector.

It seems to us truly a bit premature to speak at this moment of mandatory contributions when it has not yet been decided within the IMO what system will be adopted for this financing, and it seems to me that the European Commission also does not have a clear answer on this matter.
Finally, in point 15, the Commission is urged to clearly establish that the State with jurisdiction over waste is the responsible State.
We hardly believe it is within the Commission's competence to decide this, which must also be decided in an international agreement.

The important thing is to ensure consistency regarding the future convention on ship recycling and the Basel Convention on the transboundary control of hazardous waste and its disposal, and all of this taking into account not only the Basel Convention, but also all existing legislation.

Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues, the first thing I would like to do is congratulate the rapporteur, with whom we have a great coincidence regarding this program, and congratulate ourselves, because in times of economic crisis it is fundamental to maintain a program like this, with clear positive social effects.

only concerned with a recovery of the financial system, but rather we are fully aware that social policies, especially those that support those who need it most, the poorest in the European Union, must grow.

This food distribution program must continue to be considered part of the common agricultural policy. Here we agree with the Commission. In addition to because European agriculture has a distinct social character, because it is an instrument that works, because now it is needed more than ever, and because it must continue to function.

From the Socialist Group, just like the rapporteur, we are opposed to this program being confidential between the European Union and the Member States. Because this would imply discrimination between states according to their wealth, potentially leading to a situation where the program cannot be put into practice in the countries most in need.

It is incomprehensible that now, when it is most needed, the Commission wants to save community money in the most social aspect of the CAP. Especially considering that in all years there are budgetary surpluses in the agricultural budget.
Full community funding is fundamental for us.
We need to ensure that this program reaches all countries and especially those most in need.
43 million potential beneficiaries of this program demand a greater effort from us. And in no case should budgetary spending be reduced.
Austerity, yes, but not for the poorest.
And it is precisely to guarantee that it reaches the citizen of all member states, that the program must cover the entire journey of the distributed food.

Therefore, from the Socialist Group, for which I am the rapporteur, we have presented an amendment so that all costs arising from distribution, including transport costs, storage costs, and administrative costs, are covered by community funds.
We also agree with the rapporteur that foodstuffs should preferably be community-produced and of quality.
It is consistent with the position maintained by this Parliament in other similar programs, which we recently discussed in this chamber, as was the case with the fruit distribution plan in schools.
That's all, and thank you very much.
We hope that the Commission takes into account the position of this Parliament when it is voted on, and above all, that the plan is implemented in the European Union as soon as possible.
The Arctic region is one of the most fragile regions of our planet.
The consequences of an unrestrained exploitation of its natural resources would be catastrophic not only for the immediate environment and for the indigenous population, but for the world as a whole.

The thawing of large areas has brought these risks to the forefront, making a new regulation of global scope necessary to protect the Arctic, similar to the one that already exists for the Antarctic, even taking into account the differences that have already been highlighted in the debate.

An international treaty is necessary among all involved parties, among which the European Union is undoubtedly included, to protect its unique environment, ensure the fully sustainable nature of all types of human activities, and to multilaterally regulate navigation through the new maritime routes that are becoming accessible.
The Arctic Council, during its several years of existence, has been a model of cooperation for managing common problems.

In these moments of difficulty and uncertainty, we must elevate to higher levels that spirit and harmony to prevent the states in the area or other international actors from engaging in geostrategic struggles, forgetting what should be the common objective, the preservation of a great shared heritage.

Now, in Parliament, we tend to challenge the Commission. I believe that we are asking the Commission for an impossible mission. Because all of EU law and all the application of EU law is based on indirect application.

That is to say, the commission only has a few officials at its headquarters where it receives some complaints and has some possibilities for action. But at this moment, furthermore, the tendency is to limit budgetary powers, which means that the commission will not have the possibility to do so.
All community law, all application of community law is based on the action of national authorities: national parliaments, national judges, national officials.

Mr. President, two years ago, on a mission that was led by my colleague, my group colleague present here, Marianne, I had the opportunity to visit
Sierra Leone, and of being present in this tribunal, and of being aware of the immense work that this tribunal was carrying out, not only for Sierra Leone, but for all of humanity.
The Special Court for Sierra Leone, as has been said here, has certainly set a precedent.

A precedent because, as the resolution clearly states, it is the first international one funded by voluntary contributions. It is the first tribunal to be created in the country where the alleged crimes took place. And it is also the first tribunal, as has already been pointed out, in which a former prime minister has been tried.

For all these reasons, not only because it is a precedent, but because it is a reference for other tribunals that have been created following its example, such as Rwanda, former Yugoslavia, Cambodia, or Lebanon, we consider it essential that this resolution be approved, on which we have already been working jointly with the tribunal for several months.
Two years ago, we approved a resolution to support its funding. Two years ago, the Court was going through a difficult period, in which it was running out of money, without
money and it did not have sufficient support. Here, we must also thank the European Commission for stepping in with, shall we say, financial support for this tribunal.

Now, we ask for two things above all else. First, that convicted persons serve their sentences. What is at stake is not just the functioning of the tribunal, which is ending next year, but the legacy that this tribunal will leave us. And in the second place, obviously, all of that has to be accompanied by greater funding.
Ultimately, the Special Court for Sierra Leone is an example and is a reference for all of us and for all the tribunals that have prosecuted war crimes.

It is an example of a reference and it is a lesson that the second poorest country on this planet is giving us, in which, upon entering the court, we saw a motto written that was: without justice there is no peace.
And precisely for this reason, we have the moral obligation, not only as Europeans but as human beings, for the legacy of this tribunal to remain permanent in history.

We forget what Pufendorf was already teaching: that general laws should be few and their meaning must be understood correctly.
Those who promulgate it must employ all possible clarity.
And Montesquieu always insisted that legal language must be precise and concise.
That is to say, we must avoid esoteric verbiage, full of neologisms and unjustified linguistic borrowings, because it generates a malignant network where traps lurk and arbitrariness takes root.
We deal with very complex matters in this Parliament, but it is our obligation to make them comprehensible and accessible to the citizenry.
It is common for even legal professionals themselves, judges, lawyers, to despair when faced with our texts.
Good prose, dear colleagues, is the courtesy of the legislator and a way to demonstrate their respect for the citizen.
